text: And that's why, because of the length of this narrowing and the complexity of this narrowing, why his cardiologist decided that stent would not be as good an option or as durable an option for this patient.

I let the heart keep beating, I let his own blood keep circulating in him, and I put a special stabilizer device that they make just to make the heart stable and still in the one little area that I'm working and sew that artery onto that while it's still working.
You have an anesthesiologist and typically an anesthesia resident who's being trained or taught.
You have an anesthesia technician who's important to bring in some of the equipment and help put in the big IVs and lines that you saw.
You may have a respiratory technician too to help set up the ventilator.
We then have a circulating nurse who is an RN that goes and is kind of in charge of the room and the supplies and making sure there's good communication for whatever the scrub tech needs.
And the scrub tech is that technician who's handing me and passing me instruments and making sure that
All of the retractors that you saw and other instrumentation is prepared and ready and working properly.
I usually have an assistant like a resident, and then we have a perfusionist there.
The other thing that makes this part of the operation a little different is that it's kind of a one surgeon thing.
A lot of operations you can have an assistant across from you, but because of this institution, this part of the operation concludes that.

And that's the artery we're going to connect on to the heart to bypass everything, okay?
The result you're trying to achieve is less trauma to the patient, quicker recovery, less pain, quicker back to work or back to being able to do what he needs to do.
And so a lot of times these operations are harder on the surgeon but easier on the patient.
